title: REGIONAL GUIDELINES FOR THE DESIGN OF CONTROL MEASURES FOR STREET-VENDED FOODS (AFRICA)

sections.section: REGIONAL GUIDELINES FOR THE DESIGN OF CONTROL MEASURES FOR STREET-VENDED FOODS (AFRICA)
sections.summary: Document title and identifier for the Regional Guidelines for the Design of Control Measures for Street‑Vended Foods (Africa), CAC/GL 22R-1997 (title page/header information, not substantive requirements).
Table of contents listing the guideline's sections and subsections (contents listing/truncated), not a substantive regulatory provision.

States the Guidelines' objectives: to provide provisions for designing street food control measures and Codes of Practice in Africa and to assist authorities in upgrading street food operations to ensure availability of wholesome, safe and nutritious foods.

sections.section: SECTION II - SCOPE, USE, DEFINITION AND GENERAL REQUIREMENTS
sections.summary: Specifies scope and use: recommends general hygienic requirements for Codes of Practice for street food preparation and sale, outlines roles of relevant authorities, vendors and consumers, provides guidance on application where requirements may not be fully applicable, contains definitions of key terms, and recommends that authorities translate requirements into appropriate regulations and licensing modalities for vendors.
Specifies that the Guidelines set out general hygienic requirements and practices recommended for inclusion in Codes of Practice governing the preparation and sale of street foods.

States that each section gives objectives and the rationale in terms of food safety and suitability, and that where specific requirements are not applicable decisions should be based on what is necessary and appropriate to ensure food safety and suitability.

Provides definitions for terms used throughout the Guidelines (e.g., appliances, clean water, cleaning, contaminant/contamination, crockery, disinfection, disposable articles, establishment, food hygiene/safety/suitability, food handler, hazard, HACCP, impermeable, microorganism, mobile vendor, perishable/readily perishable, ready-to-eat, potable water referencing WHO Guidelines, primary production, relevant authority, sealed container, street food centre/stall, waste water and water container).

Requires relevant authorities to prepare appropriate regulations (or incorporate into existing food regulations), to translate hygienic requirements into Codes of Practice that account for local conditions and risks, and to establish modalities for issuance and renewal of vendor licences.

sections.section: SECTION III - ESTABLISHMENT: DESIGN, FACILITIES, MAINTENANCE AND SANITATION
sections.summary: Section III sets objectives and rationale for establishment design, facilities, maintenance and sanitation to minimise contamination, permit effective cleaning/disinfection, ensure food-contact materials are non-toxic and durable, provide necessary environmental controls and pest protection, and it details requirements on location, premises, equipment, facilities, maintenance, cleaning, pest control and monitoring.

Requires establishments and stalls (including mobile vendors) to be sited away from likely contamination sources—polluted or flood-prone areas without safeguards, pest-prone areas, and places with ineffective waste removal—be located in areas designated by the relevant authority, and keep surrounding spaces free of unnecessary stored or discarded items to permit cleaning.

Mandates vendor stalls be of a type approved by the relevant authority and that premises be constructed of durable, easy-to-clean, non-toxic, impervious materials (walls, floors, ceilings, windows, doors and food-contact surfaces), with working surfaces and equipment easy to clean/disinfect and cooking ranges, washing equipment, tables, shelves and cupboards at least 45 cm above ground; temporary/mobile premises must be sited and designed to minimise contamination and pest harbourage.

Requires food-contact equipment and containers (except single‑use) to be non-toxic, cleanable, and where necessary disassemblable for cleaning/inspection; mandates separation of cooked and uncooked food utensils and routine cleaning (wash with detergent then either immerse 0.5 minute in boiling water or 2 minutes in potable water at not less than 77°C, with a less-preferred potable-water rinse alternative if hot water unavailable), separate storage of clean and used utensils, clean protection for condiment containers, disposable hand-service items, identifiable impervious waste containers (dangerous substances labelled/lockable), and removal and discard of defective appliances and crockery.

Requires an adequate potable water supply meeting WHO drinking-water guidance (with separate identified non-potable systems where used), adequate drainage and waste disposal to avoid contamination, designated cleaning facilities with hot/cold potable water, maintained washbasins and handwashing facilities (soap), personnel hygiene facilities (lavatories, changing rooms) suitably located, adequate protected lighting, and appropriate storage for food, ingredients and hazardous non-food chemicals (separate secure storage where necessary).

Mandates establishments and equipment be kept in good repair to facilitate sanitation and prevent contamination; cleaning must remove residues and may be followed by disinfection, with cleaning chemicals handled/stored per manufacturers' instructions, and allows physical and chemical cleaning methods with steps including gross debris removal, detergent application, rinsing with compliant water, dry cleaning where appropriate, and disinfection when necessary.

Requires cleaning and disinfection programmes covering all parts of the establishment (including cleaning equipment) to be continually monitored for suitability and effectiveness and documented where necessary, and specifies that written programmes should list areas/equipment to be cleaned, responsibilities, methods and frequency, and monitoring arrangements, with expert consultation as appropriate.

Sets pest-control requirements: employ good hygiene, sanitation and inspection to minimise infestation risk; maintain buildings to prevent pest access and eliminate breeding sites; store potential food sources in pest‑proof ways; monitor for infestation; and promptly eradicate pests using treatments (chemical, physical or biological) that do not compromise food safety or suitability.

Requires suitable provision for removal and storage of waste so it does not accumulate in food handling, storage or working areas except where unavoidable, and mandates that waste stores be kept appropriately clean.

Specifies sanitation duties for vendors: ensure sufficient potable water (stored in clean containers if required), provide efficient waste-water systems with traps, remove solid waste from working areas at least daily into covered containers and clean/disinfect receptacles and storage areas as required, clean working surfaces and surrounding areas at least daily, and ensure access to authority‑approved, clean toilet facilities.

Requires sanitation systems to be monitored for effectiveness and periodically verified (e.g., audits, pre-operational inspections or, where appropriate, microbiological sampling of environment and food-contact surfaces) and regularly reviewed and adapted as circumstances change.

Defines street food centres that relevant authorities should provide or approve to give vendors adequate facilities and utilities (potable water, lighting, drainage, waste disposal), safe storage/preparation/serving space, a setting for education and training of managers, vendors and consumers, and support for vendor associations, and states that vendors in such centres must comply with applicable regulations and location/design requirements (per Section 3.1).
States that all vendors located in a street food centre must comply with all provisions of the officially recognized regulations applicable to street food vendors.

Requires the location of any street food centre to be pre-approved by the relevant authority in accordance with Section 3.1 (Location) and that the centre's design meet specified hygienic and construction requirements.
